content.text: The KV cache eliminates redundant computation during autoregressive decoding. Without it, generating the
-th token requires a full forward pass over all

previous tokens — an O(n²) total cost across all decode steps. With the cache, each step is a single forward pass over one new token that attends to cached keys and values, reducing total decoding cost to O(n). The practical speedup for generating a 512-token response from a 1024-token prompt is not 2× but dramatically higher, because the recomputed prefix dominates the uncached case.
Why Speedup Scales with Sequence Length
tokens from a prompt of length
requires forward passes over contexts of length
With the cache, the prefill processes
tokens once, then each of the
decode steps processes exactly 1 new token:
For long prompts or large generation lengths, the speedup ratio
— generating 1000 tokens is 1000× faster with cache than without (in raw compute terms).

import time
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

prompt = "The history of artificial intelligence begins" * 5   # ~50 tokens
input_ids = tok.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
MAX_NEW = 100

def benchmark(use_cache: bool, runs: int = 3) -> float:
    times = []
    for _ in range(runs):
        with torch.no_grad():
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            model.generate(
                input_ids,
                max_new_tokens=MAX_NEW,
                do_sample=False,
                use_cache=use_cache,
            )
            times.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)
    return sum(times) / len(times)

t_no_cache = benchmark(use_cache=False)
t_cached   = benchmark(use_cache=True)

print(f"Prompt length:     {input_ids.shape[1]} tokens")
print(f"New tokens:        {MAX_NEW}")
print(f"No-cache time:     {t_no_cache:.3f}s")
print(f"With-cache time:   {t_cached:.3f}s")
print(f"Speedup:           {t_no_cache / t_cached:.1f}×")
print(f"Tokens/sec (cache): {MAX_NEW / t_cached:.1f}")

# Validate outputs are identical
with torch.no_grad():
    out_nc = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=MAX_NEW, do_sample=False, use_cache=False)
    out_c  = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=MAX_NEW, do_sample=False, use_cache=True)
assert (out_nc == out_c).all(), "outputs differ — logic error"
print("Outputs match: True")
The two phases have different performance characteristics. Prefill is
: many tokens are processed in parallel with large matrix multiplications that saturate GPU cores. Decode is

: each step processes one token but must read the entire model weight and KV cache from GPU HBM. This bottleneck explains why decode tokens/sec is typically much lower than prefill tokens/sec, and why techniques that reduce KV reads (GQA, speculative decoding) improve decode throughput disproportionately.

import torch
import time
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

# Measure prefill speed: process N tokens all at once
def measure_prefill(n_tokens: int, runs: int = 5) -> float:
    dummy = torch.randint(0, tok.vocab_size, (1, n_tokens))
    times = []
    for _ in range(runs):
        with torch.no_grad():
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            _ = model(dummy, use_cache=True)
            times.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)
    return n_tokens / (sum(times) / len(times))  # tokens/sec

# Measure decode speed: generate one token at a time from a cached prefix
def measure_decode(prefix_len: int, n_new: int = 50, runs: int = 3) -> float:
    prefix = torch.randint(0, tok.vocab_size, (1, prefix_len))
    times = []
    for _ in range(runs):
        with torch.no_grad():
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            model.generate(
                prefix, max_new_tokens=n_new, do_sample=False, use_cache=True
            )
        times.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)
    # subtract prefill time (first step); approximate as total/n_new
    return n_new / (sum(times) / len(times))

print("Prefill throughput:")
for n in [64, 256, 512]:
    tps = measure_prefill(n)
    print(f"  {n:4d} tokens: {tps:7.1f} tok/s")

print("\nDecode throughput (cached):")
for n in [64, 256, 512]:
    tps = measure_decode(n)
    print(f"  prefix={n:4d}: {tps:7.1f} tok/s")

# Prefill is typically 5–20× faster than decode on modern hardware
# because it runs large batched matrix multiplications vs single-token reads

The KV cache converts autoregressive generation from a quadratic to a linear algorithm. In practice the measured wall-clock speedup is lower than the theoretical maximum because the decode phase is memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound — reading the cache from HBM is the bottleneck, not the matmuls. But the cache is still the non-negotiable foundation of production LLM serving: every serious inference framework (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, DeepSpeed-MII) assumes it. The only reason to disable it is in low-memory environments where the cache itself would exhaust GPU memory before the generation completes.

Without the KV cache, deploying a large language model for interactive use is economically infeasible. Consider serving GPT-2 (1.5B parameters) with a 512-token prompt generating 256 tokens: the uncached path processes 512+1, 512+2, ..., 512+256 = 256 forward passes over contexts totalling more than 131,000 tokens. On a modern GPU that processes ~100 tokens/ms in the prefill phase, this takes over 1.3 seconds — unacceptable latency for an interactive chat application. With the cache, the same generation requires one prefill pass (512 tokens, ~5ms) plus 256 decode steps of 1 token each (~2.56ms at ~100 decode tokens/s), totalling under 10ms. The cache reduces the problem from quadratic to linear in the number of generated tokens.

Production LLM services (ChatGPT, Claude.ai, Gemini) all rely on the KV cache as a non-negotiable infrastructure component. Without it, serving a single user generating a 1,000-token response from a 2,000-token context would require the same compute as processing 1,500 full documents — a 1,500× cost amplification. The speedup is not merely a convenience optimisation; it is what makes conversational AI commercially viable at scale.
Total decode cost drops from O(G²/2 + G·P) to O(P + G) where P is prompt length and G is the number of generated tokens. For G=1000 and P=1000, this is a ~500× reduction in FLOPs.
First-token latency (TTFT) is determined by the prefill phase alone. With caching, the decode phase does not re-process the prompt, keeping TTFT stable regardless of generation length.

Cloud APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI) cache the KV activations of long system prompts across API calls. Requests that reuse the same system prompt skip the prefill for that prefix, reducing cost and latency by 60–90% in prompt-heavy workflows.
The cached computation is mathematically identical to the uncached computation — caching is a pure optimisation with no accuracy trade-off.

Speculative decoding (draft model proposes multiple tokens, target model verifies in parallel) relies on the KV cache to efficiently score candidate continuations. Without the cache, verification would be as expensive as generation.

The cache stores K and V tensors for every layer, every head, and every cached token. For a 70B-parameter model with 80 layers, 64 heads, head dimension 128, and a 32K-token context, the KV cache at float16 requires 80 × 2 × 64 × 128 × 32000 × 2 bytes ≈ 84 GB — comparable to the model weights themselves. This memory pressure limits batch size and maximum context length.

Decode steps are memory-bandwidth-bound: each step reads the entire KV cache from GPU HBM to the compute units. As the cache grows with generation length, decode throughput (tokens/s) degrades. For a 100K-token context on an A100 (900 GB/s HBM bandwidth), reading the KV cache alone takes ~93 ms per decode step for a 70B model.

When processing many short independent prompts (e.g., embedding computation), the KV cache provides no speedup because each input is a self-contained prefill with no shared prefix. Large-batch prefill is compute-bound and benefits from batching, not caching.

Beam search with beam width B requires storing B separate KV caches. Tree-attention and paged attention (PagedAttention, vLLM) mitigate this with shared prefix storage, but branching always increases memory relative to single-sequence generation.
When to Use (and When Not to Use) the KV Cache
Eliminates O(G²) decode cost; no trade-offs
No decode phase — cache provides no benefit, wastes memory
Cache memory may exceed available RAM; disable if generating <50 tokens
Very long context (100K+ tokens) on a small GPU
Cache may exhaust VRAM; use quantised KV cache (int8/int4) or paged attention
How to Implement Prompt Caching (Anthropic API)

Anthropic's API supports prompt caching via the cache_control parameter. Marking a content block with {'cache_control': {'type': 'ephemeral'}} instructs the API to cache the KV activations for that prefix. Subsequent requests with the same prefix (up to the cache breakpoint) skip the prefill computation for the cached portion, typically reducing latency by 60–80% and cost by 90% for cached tokens:

import anthropic
import time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Simulate a long system document (e.g., a codebase, legal document)
LONG_DOCUMENT = """
PRODUCT SPECIFICATION — DataPipeline v3.2
" + "=" * 60 + "

Architecture Overview:
The DataPipeline system consists of four core components:
1. Ingestion Layer: Accepts data from REST, gRPC, Kafka, and S3
2. Validation Layer: Schema validation with Pydantic models
3. Transform Layer: Apache Beam transforms with Python SDK
4. Storage Layer: Writes to BigQuery, PostgreSQL, or Parquet

Performance Requirements:
- Throughput: 100,000 events/second per shard
- Latency (p99): < 500ms end-to-end
- Availability: 99.9% uptime SLA
- Data retention: 90 days hot, 7 years cold archive

[...15,000 more tokens of documentation...]
"""

# Question set — all share the same long context
questions = [
    "What is the p99 latency requirement?",
    "What storage backends does the system support?",
    "How many events per second can each shard process?",
]

def ask_with_cache(question: str, model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001") -> tuple[str, float]:
    """Send a question against the cached document and return (answer, elapsed_seconds)."""
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=200,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": "You are a technical analyst. Answer questions concisely based on the documentation provided.",
            },
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": LONG_DOCUMENT,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # Cache this prefix
            },
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    answer = response.content[0].text.strip()

    # Inspect cache usage
    usage = response.usage
    if hasattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens"):
        print(f"Cache read: {usage.cache_read_input_tokens} tokens, "
              f"Cache created: {getattr(usage, 'cache_creation_input_tokens', 0)} tokens")
    return answer, elapsed

print("First request (cache population):")
ans, t = ask_with_cache(questions[0])
print(f"  Q: {questions[0]}")
print(f"  A: {ans}")
print(f"  Time: {t:.2f}s")

print("\nSubsequent requests (cache hits):")
for q in questions[1:]:
    ans, t = ask_with_cache(q)
    print(f"  Q: {q}")
    print(f"  A: {ans}")
    print(f"  Time: {t:.2f}s (should be faster than first request)")

print("\nNote: Prompt cache hits reduce input token costs by 90% on Anthropic API")

The cache trades compute for memory. In batch serving, enabling the cache increases per-sequence memory by tens of GB for long contexts, reducing the maximum batch size. Profile both latency and throughput (requests/second) before assuming the cache improves overall system performance.

HuggingFace's model.generate() enables the cache by default (use_cache=True). Passing use_cache=False accidentally — perhaps to debug an output — quadratically degrades performance and should never happen in production.

For very long contexts, storing the KV cache in float16 exhausts GPU memory. KV cache quantisation to int8 or int4 reduces memory by 2–4× with minimal accuracy loss (typically < 0.5 perplexity points) and is available in most inference frameworks.

The KV cache primarily improves throughput (tokens/s per sequence) during decoding. It does not improve first-token latency for fresh prompts — that is determined by prefill speed. Optimise prefill (larger batch, tensor parallelism) and decode (KV cache, quantisation) separately.
Always enable the KV cache for generation:

There is no scenario in which disabling the cache improves performance for autoregressive generation. The only valid reason to disable it is to debug correctness by verifying that cached and uncached outputs are identical.
Use paged attention for variable-length batches:
PagedAttention (vLLM) stores KV cache in non-contiguous memory pages, eliminating fragmentation and enabling near-optimal GPU utilisation for batches with varying sequence lengths.
Quantise the KV cache for long contexts:
Use int8 KV cache quantisation (available in TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, llama.cpp) for contexts > 8K tokens. Measure perplexity before deploying to confirm accuracy is acceptable for your task.
Place stable system prompts first for prefix caching:

When using cloud APIs with prompt caching (Anthropic, OpenAI), put static content (system instructions, reference documents) before dynamic content (user messages). The cache breakpoint must be at the prefix boundary for caching to activate.
Simple API; DynamicCache and SlidingWindowCache classes
CPU and consumer GPU inference; minimal dependencies
90% cost reduction on cached tokens; automatic for long system prompts

KV cache implementations carry security implications in multi-tenant serving environments. If a server reuses KV cache memory pages without zeroing them (as an optimisation), a new request might read stale activations from a previous user's conversation — a side-channel attack that leaks private context. Production serving frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM) explicitly zero cache pages on allocation, but custom implementations must do the same. Prompt caching APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI) cache per user/API key by default and do not share cache entries across accounts.
Always zero KV cache memory blocks before assigning them to a new sequence. Memory reuse without zeroing is a confidentiality vulnerability in multi-tenant deployments.
In shared infrastructure, KV cache must be tenant-isolated. Prefix caching across users is only safe if cached prefixes contain no user-specific information (e.g., a universal system prompt).

Long generations can exhaust KV cache memory mid-generation, causing an OOM error. Implement graceful degradation — either pre-compute maximum context lengths and reject requests that would exceed cache capacity, or use streaming eviction (sliding window attention) for unbounded generation.
The KV cache converts autoregressive generation from O(G²) to O(P + G) total compute by caching key and value tensors for all previously seen tokens, so each decode step processes only the new token.
Speedup grows proportionally with generation length G and prompt length P — generating 1,000 tokens from a 1,000-token prompt is approximately 500× cheaper with cache than without in raw FLOPs.
The decode phase is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound. Reading the KV cache from GPU HBM is the bottleneck; techniques that reduce cache reads (GQA, quantisation) improve decode throughput.
Prompt caching across API requests amortises the prefill cost of repeated system prompts, reducing input token costs by up to 90% on Anthropic and OpenAI APIs.
Memory is the main trade-off: KV cache for a 70B model with a 32K context requires ~84 GB — comparable to the model weights. GQA and KV quantisation reduce this by 8–32× and 2–4× respectively.
Always enable the KV cache for autoregressive generation; disable it only to verify correctness by confirming that cached and uncached outputs are identical.
In multi-tenant deployments, zero KV cache memory before reuse and enforce tenant isolation for prefix caches to prevent cross-user information leakage.
